passages.title: Neanderthal Technology
passages.source_html:
Neanderthal Technology


A


We think of our prehistoric ancestors as people of the ice and snow, living in caves, and for many of the west European Neanderthalers that is a just picture of their life. But where there were no caves, further to the east on the Russian steppe, for example, open-air sites with some sort of constructed shelter were the only option.


We now know much more about the cave sites than the open-air ones because, historically, it was the cave sites of Western Europe that were first explored by archaeologists and also because open-air sites are harder to find-many of them have disappeared under deep mud deposits or under the rising postglacial seas. Caves, moreover, aid the survival of archaeological material and can preserve the records of remote millennia.


B


In south-west France, the limestone caves of the Périgord region made ideal homes for the Neanderthal people. There were good supplies of flint to hand for axes and the like, and the caves were often sited in small river valleys that offered protection against the worst of the weather. The Neanderthalers liked south-facing caves, for obvious reasons of sunshine and wind avoidance, and caves at some height above the valley floor offered refuge from floods and good game-watching vantage.


The Périgord region during the last ice age was, in fact, an exceptionally benign habitat for humans. It enjoyed a rather maritime climate with cooler summers that permitted the extension of tundra and steppe over its higher plateaux, and its year-round high levels of sunshine favoured the growth of the ground plants needed by reindeer, bison and horse. Winters were mildish for the ice age, animals never needed to migrate far from summer to winter, and men never needed to travel far from home to find abundant supplies of meat.


C


In Central and Eastern Europe, where caves were unavailable, such open-air sites as have been discovered were mostly located near water – both because this was a good area to be for people and animals, and also because the sedimentation potential of lakes and stream course has aided archaeological preservation – whereas erosion has presumably blown away sites which were out in the open. Some of the open-air sites in Germany, Central Europe and Russia have provided valuable information about Neanderthal man and his way of life. From Moldova, for example, comes evidence that has been interpreted as the remains of wind-break structures, or even a large tent: a ring, up to about 8 x 5m in size, of mainly mammoth bones enclosing a dense concentration of stone tools, animal bones and ash.


D


From the west European caves more evidence of built structures is available, and some of it goes back a long way in time .In the Grotte du Lazaret, near Nice, at a date during the last ice age but one, claims for some sort of skin tent within the cave have been advanced, on the basis of arrangements of large stones out from the cave wall that might have supported timber struts for a covering of skins up to the rock face above.


At Lazaret, what might be openings in the hypothesised tents seem to point away from the cave mouth, and finds of wolf and fox foot bones, without the rest of the skeletons, inside these 'tents' have been thought to indicate the use of animal pelts as bed coverings. The two patches of ash at Lazaret that mark ancient fires, with stone tools around them evidently made and used on the spot, are edged with small marine molluse shells, prompting the excavator to suggest that seaweed had been used as bedding around the fires. The cave of Baume-Bonne in the Basses-Alpes region of France, another early site, boasts ten square metres of cobbles brought up from the local river and laid down, as though to take care of a puddle area in the cave, with the smoothest and roundest surfaces of the stones uppermost, and there are other similar cases.


E


The ash encountered in concentrations at some sites testifies to the Neanderthal people's use of fire: not surprising, since use of fire was, by Neanderthal times, an already ancient accomplishment of evolving humanity, and survival in the sub-arctic conditions faced by the Neanderthalers is inconceivable without control of fire. Fire gave warmth, light, heat for cooking and defence against predatory animals. A charred piece of birch from Krapina in Croatiatzx, is thought to be the remains of a fire-making twirl stick. But Neanderthal hearths, in the sense of specially constructed places for fire, are fewer and harder to identify with certainty than the mere ash piles that are a regular feature of their sites. They seem often to have just lit a small fire (40-50cm across) on the existing ground surface of the cave, without preparation. Judging from the shallow penetration of heat effects under the ash, this fire was only of a short duration. Sometimes the fires were larger in size, up to one metre across, and quite irregular in shape. It is not always easy to decide how much additional structure some fires possessed: claims of stone circles to contain the fire run up against the fact that stones tend to litter the cave floors everywhere and those around a fire can quite accidentally look as though they were arranged in a circle.

groups.title: Questions 27-40
groups.source_html:
Questions 27 - 31


The text has five sections. Choose the correct heading for each section and move it into the gap.

Options (Word Bank):
A. Evidence of outdoor dwellings
B. Learning to make fire
C. A perfect place to live
D. Examining the cave contents
E. Contrasting two types of home
F. A vital source of power
27
正确答案： 27 E. Contrasting two types of home  |  28 C. A perfect place to live  |  29 A. Evidence of outdoor dwellings  |  30 D. Examining the cave contents  |  31 F. A vital source of power
Question 27

定位信息：Section A    


对应原文：A段，We think of our prehistoric ancestors as people of the ice and snow, living in caves, and for many of the west European Neanderthalers that is a just picture of their life. But where there were no caves, further to the east on the Russian steppe, for example, open-air sites with some sort of constructed shelter were the only option.    


解题思路：A段指出，很多西欧尼安德特人居住在洞穴中，而在没有洞穴的东欧草原上，他们只能选择露天的临时住所。这形成了对两种不同类型住所的对比。    

Question 28

定位信息：Section B    


对应原文：B段 ，In south-west France, the limestone caves of the Périgord region made ideal homes for the Neanderthal people.以及The Périgord region during the last ice age was, in fact, an exceptionally benign habitat for humans.   


解题思路：B段指出了Périgord地区的洞穴如何为尼安德特人提供理想的居住环境，包括天气、食物供应和安全保护。


同义替换：perfect=ideal; place to live=habitat    

Question 29

定位信息：Section C    


对应原文：C段，In Central and Eastern Europe, where caves were unavailable, such open-air sites as have been discovered were mostly located near water – both because this was a good area to be for people and animals, and also because the sedimentation potential of lakes and stream course has aided archaeological preservation – whereas erosion has presumably blown away sites which were out in the open. Some of the open-air sites in Germany, Central Europe and Russia have provided valuable information about Neanderthal man and his way of life. 


解题思路： C段指出了中欧和东欧地区发现的露天居住遗址，指出这些露天遗址为尼安德特人的生活方式提供了宝贵信息。段落内容主要强调了露天居所的存在证据。    

Question 30

定位信息：Section D    


对应原文：D段全文    


解题思路：D段指出了法国Lazaret洞穴中的一些构造物，推测可能是皮制帐篷，并详细描述了大石块的排列方式可能用来支撑木头结构。这些内容显示了考古学家对洞穴内构造物的研究。    

Question 31

定位信息：Section E 


对应原文：E段，The ash encountered in concentrations at some sites testifies to the Neanderthal people's use of fire: not surprising, since use of fire was, by Neanderthal times, an already ancient accomplishment of evolving humanity, and survival in the sub-arctic conditions faced by the Neanderthalers is inconceivable without control of fire. 


解题思路：E段指出了火对尼安德特人生活的重要性，特别是在寒冷条件下提供温暖、光亮、烹饪食物和防御掠食者的能力。段落强调了火作为一种“关键的生存力量”的重要性。   

 

同义替换：source of power=use of fire    

Questions 32 - 36


Choose the correct place, (A - E), for each finding. You may choose any correct place more than once.


List of Places

A The Périgord region

B Moldova

C The Grotte du Lazaret

D The cave of Baume-Bonne

E Krapina


Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
32

32 a burnt piece of wood
✅ 正确答案：E

定位信息：Krapina


对应原文： E段，"A charred piece of birch from Krapina in Croatiatzx, is thought to be the remains of a fire-making twirl stick. "


解题思路：E段指出在Krapina发现了一块烧焦的桦木，可能是取火用的工具。


同义替换：burnt=charred
33

33 evidence of efforts to prevent pools of water forming
✅ 正确答案：D

定位信息： The cave of Baume-Bonne


对应原文： D段："The cave of Baume-Bonne in the Basses-Alpes region of France, another early site, boasts ten square metres of cobbles brought up from the local river and laid down, as though to take care of a puddle area in the cave, with the smoothest and roundest surfaces of the stones uppermost, and there are other similar cases."


解题思路：D段指出在Baume-Bonne洞穴中发现了鹅卵石，似乎是为了防止水坑的形成。


同义替换：prevent pools of water forming=take care of a puddle area
34

34 the remains of sea creatures
✅ 正确答案：C

定位信息：The Grotte du Lazaret


对应原文： D段，"The two patches of ash at Lazaret that mark ancient fires, with stone tools around them evidently made and used on the spot, are edged with small marine molluse shells, prompting the excavator to suggest that seaweed had been used as bedding around the fires."


解题思路：D段指出在Lazaret洞穴的灰烬旁发现了海洋软体动物的贝壳。


同义替换：sea creatures=marine mollusc shells
35

35 a circular arrangement of animal bones
✅ 正确答案：B

定位信息： Moldova


对应原文： C段："From Moldova, for example, comes evidence that has been interpreted as the remains of wind-break structures, or even a large tent: a ring, up to about 8 x 5m in size, of mainly mammoth bones enclosing a dense concentration of stone tools, animal bones and ash."


解题思路：C段指出在Moldova发现的圆形猛犸骨排列。


同义替换：circular arrangement=a ring
36

36 evidence suggesting the use of animal fur for warmth
✅ 正确答案：C

定位信息：The Grotte du Lazaret


对应原文： D段：" At Lazaret, what might be openings in the hypothesised tents seem to point away from the cave mouth, and finds of wolf and fox foot bones, without the rest of the skeletons, inside these 'tents' have been thought to indicate the use of animal pelts as bed coverings."


解题思路：D段指出在Lazaret洞穴发现的狼和狐狸骨骼暗示动物皮毛被用作床上覆盖物。


同义替换：fur=pelts

Questions 37 - 39


Complete the summary. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS in each gap.

37

The use of fire

Neanderthalers could not have survived without fire because the conditions they lived in were  . Most evidence of purpose-built fires takes the form of ash piles, features of which suggest that the fires lasted a  time. It is hard to be certain about the size and structure of the fires, though they were certainly needed to protect the occupants from dangerous  among other things.

正确答案： 37 sub-arctic  |  38 short  |  39 predatory animals
Question 37

定位信息： survived without fire,conditions


对应原文： E段，"The ash encountered in concentrations at some sites testifies to the Neanderthal people's use of fire: not surprising, since use of fire was, by Neanderthal times, an already ancient accomplishment of evolving humanity, and survival in the sub-arctic conditions faced by the Neanderthalers is inconceivable without control of fire."


解题思路：E段指出尼安德特人生活在“sub-arctic”条件下，没有火是无法生存的。

Question 38

定位信息： ash piles,fires lasted


对应原文： E段："Judging from the shallow penetration of heat effects under the ash, this fire was only of a short duration."


解题思路：E段指出这些火焰持续的时间是短暂的。


同义替换：a short time=short duration


Question 39

定位信息： protect from dangerous,animals


对应原文： E段："Fire gave warmth, light, heat for cooking and defence against predatory animals."


解题思路：E段指出火的用途之一是防御掠食性动物。

Questions 40


Choose the correct answer.

40

The purpose of the writer of this article is to

正确答案： C. discuss what is known about Neanderthal settlements.

定位信息: purpose of the writer

对应原文:A段， We now know much more about the cave sites than the open-air ones because, historically, it was the cave sites of Western Europe that were first explored by archaeologists and also because open-air sites are harder to find-many of them have disappeared under deep mud deposits or under the rising postglacial seas. 

B段，In south-west France, the limestone caves of the Périgord region made ideal homes for the Neanderthal people. 

C段，In Central and Eastern Europe, where caves were unavailable, such open-air sites as have been discovered were mostly located near water – both because this was a good area to be for people and animals, and also because the sedimentation potential of lakes and stream course has aided archaeological preservation – whereas erosion has presumably blown away sites which were out in the open.

D段，From the west European caves more evidence of built structures is available, and some of it goes back a long way in time .

E段，The ash encountered in concentrations at some sites testifies to the Neanderthal people's use of fire: not surprising, since use of fire was, by Neanderthal times, an already ancient accomplishment of evolving humanity, and survival in the sub-arctic conditions faced by the Neanderthalers is inconceivable without control of fire. 


解题思路：文章主要探讨了尼安德特人的住所形态，包括西欧的洞穴住所（A段和B段）和中东欧的露天住所（C段），并补充了与居住相关的考古发现（D段）以及生活方式的细节（E段）这些讨论都集中在展示当前关于尼安德特人定居点的已知信息，而非关注住所大小、迁移原因或发展过程。
